Hollywood Writers End Strike
----------------------------

om/business/business-news/writers-guild-strike-end-1235600992/ ) :

Studios, represented by the Alliance of Motion Picture and Television Produ=

overy=E2=80=99s David Zaslav and NBCUniversal=E2=80=99s Donna Langley) atte=

There are two interesting points to make about the resolution of this strik=
e, captured in the last two sentences of the excerpt. Start with AI; from t=
he Wall Street Journal ( https://www.wsj.com/business/media/hollywood-studi=
os-can-train-ai-models-on-writers-work-under-tentative-deal-aedae589 ) :

lligence models based on writers=E2=80=99 work under the terms of a tentati=
ve labor agreement between the two sides, people familiar with the situatio=
n said. The writers would also walk away with an important win, a guarantee=

provision had been in an earlier offer from the Alliance of Motion Picture =
and Television Producers, the group representing studios, streamers and net=

AI use does not appear to be limited in any way (for either studios or writ=
ers), but AI will not be considered a writer, which has a bunch of knock-on=
 effects:

* If an AI rewrites a writer=E2=80=99s script, that writer is still conside=
red the sole writer and doesn=E2=80=99t need to share residuals with the AI=
.
* If a writer rewrites an AI script, the rewrite is considered an original =
script by the writer.
* If a writer is asked to create a script from AI-generated material and co=
ncepts, that must be disclosed to the writer; at the same time, the writer=
=E2=80=99s work is considered original, and not =E2=80=9Cassigned material=
=E2=80=9D, which reduces residuals.

In short, studios will gain absolutely no economic benefit from using gener=
ative AI, at least in terms of union-defined jobs; writers will get paid as=
 if the AI doesn=E2=80=99t exist. Instead, what the studios appear to be th=
e most interested in is the creative benefit of AI. They want to be able to=
 generate ideas for free, even if they have to pay to have those ideas fine=
-tuned into scripts by union-covered writers.

This strikes me as a big win for studios. The saying goes that ideas are a =
dime-a-dozen, but that still requires some sort of human-limited production=
 function; now the studios can generate an infinite number of ideas for eff=
ectively zero cost. Yes, this will mean a lot of dreck, but as long as the =
absolute number of viable ideas is sufficiently high, the cost in time and =
effort to wade through that dreck will likely be less than the prices comma=
nded by truly innovative show-runners who come up with ideas that are execu=
ted by union writers today. In other words, the union, as you might expect,=
 protected the commodity writer, but has left the truly differentiated writ=
er exposed. That is, I suppose, what unions do, which is why I=E2=80=99ve a=
lways been skeptical as to why any differentiated writer would want to join=

The second interesting point is about the WGA=E2=80=99s claim that the agre=
ement is =E2=80=9Cexceptional=E2=80=9D, a description that was echoed by mo=

 all of those concessions are in the context of an individual show; unsurpr=
